lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: What does the guide RNA do in the CRISPR-Cas9 system?
choices: It cuts the DNA strands directly
It tells Cas9 which DNA sequence to cut
It repairs the DNA after cutting
It builds new proteins from scratch
why: The guide RNA carries a ~20-letter sequence that matches the target DNA — it's the address. Cas9 is the enzyme that does the actual cutting once the guide leads it to the spot.

lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: What did this 2012 paper actually demonstrate?
choices: Curing a genetic disease in a patient
Editing genes inside living human beings
Programmable, targeted DNA cutting in a test tube
That CRISPR exists in bacteria for the first time
why: The study showed Cas9 could be reprogrammed with a custom guide to cut chosen DNA sequences in a tube. Working inside living cells and treating patients were later advances built on this result.

lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: Why was this a leap over earlier gene-editing methods?
choices: It required no DNA at all
You reprogram the target by writing a short RNA instead of engineering a new protein each time
It was the first method that could cut DNA
It eliminated all off-target mistakes
why: Earlier approaches needed a custom protein built for every target — slow and expensive. CRISPR-Cas9 lets you retarget by ordering a short, cheap guide RNA. Off-target cuts remained a real challenge to solve later.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: In a typical prospect-theory gamble, people offered a sure $500 versus a coin-flip for $1,000 or nothing tend to do what?
choices: Take the gamble, because it could pay double
Take the sure $500, playing it safe with gains
Refuse to answer either way
Split evenly — the model predicts indifference
why: Both options average $500, so a pure calculator would be indifferent. But for gains, real people are risk-averse and grab the sure thing — one of the clean breaks from the rational model.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: What does 'loss aversion' say about how a $100 loss compares to a $100 gain?
choices: They feel exactly equal in size
The gain feels stronger because we're optimists
The loss hurts roughly twice as much as the gain feels good
Neither registers unless the amount is large
why: Kahneman and Tversky found losses 'loom larger' than gains, with the pain of a loss running about twice the pleasure of an equal gain — a ratio later estimated near 2.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: Why can the same final dollar amount feel like a gain to one person and a loss to another?
choices: Because of differences in expected utility
Because they have different reference points
Because probabilities are subjective
Because one of them did the math wrong
why: Prospect theory says we judge outcomes relative to a reference point — usually our current position. Ending at $1,000 feels like a win if you started below it and a loss if you started above it.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: Why did scientists suspect the body made its own cannabis-like molecule?
choices: Because cannabis is a very common plant
Because the brain has a receptor that THC binds to, and the body wouldn't build a lock without a natural key
Because people reported feeling high without using cannabis
Because THC is found naturally in the human body
why: The discovery of a cannabinoid receptor implied a natural molecule meant to fit it — the body does not build receptors for a plant it may never encounter. That natural key turned out to be anandamide.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: What does the name 'anandamide' come from?
choices: A scientist named Ananda
The Greek word for brain
The Sanskrit word ananda, meaning bliss or joy
The chemical company that funded the study
why: Mechoulam's team named the molecule after the Sanskrit word ananda, meaning bliss — a nod to the cannabinoid receptor it binds to.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: Which statement best reflects a limitation noted in the discovery?
choices: Anandamide is extremely stable and easy to make into a drug
Anandamide breaks down within minutes and is only one of multiple endocannabinoids
Anandamide is the only molecule that binds the cannabinoid receptor
Anandamide proved cannabis has no effect on the brain
why: Anandamide is fragile and degrades quickly, and a second, more abundant endocannabinoid (2-AG) was later identified — so anandamide is one piece of a larger system, and turning the finding into stable medicine remains a work in progress.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: What was the ONLY major difference between the two groups of mice?
choices: One group ate fewer calories
One group ate a low-fat diet
One group could only eat within an 8-hour window
One group exercised more
why: Both groups ate the same high-fat food and the same number of calories. The single variable the researchers changed was timing: free grazing versus an 8-hour daily window.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: What does 'circadian rhythm' refer to?
choices: A type of low-calorie diet
The body's internal ~24-hour clock that governs processes like sleep and digestion
A heart-rate measurement
An exercise routine done at the same time daily
why: Circadian rhythm is your built-in roughly 24-hour clock. The study's big idea is that organs like the liver follow it, so eating at the wrong hours throws metabolism off.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: Why does the lesson urge caution about applying this to people?
choices: The mice were given a drug, not just a schedule
It was a mouse study, and a major human trial (TREAT, 2020) found the eating window alone gave no extra weight loss
The mice actually gained more weight
The study was never published
why: It was done in mice, whose metabolism differs from ours. Human results have been mixed — the 2020 TREAT trial found an 8-hour window alone wasn't more effective for weight loss than eating across the day.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: What was the key change the Transformer made versus older translation systems?
choices: It read text strictly one word at a time
It looked at all the words at once and weighed which mattered most
It used a much larger dictionary
It translated only between English and French
why: Older systems processed words in order. The Transformer's attention mechanism lets the model consider every word at the same time and decide which ones matter for understanding each one — which also let the work run in parallel.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: Why does 'in parallel' matter so much for training AI?
choices: It makes the model more polite
It lets many calculations run at once, fitting how modern AI chips work
It reduces the number of words the model needs
It guarantees a correct translation
why: Doing the work in parallel means many operations run simultaneously instead of single file. AI chips are built for exactly that, so the Transformer trained faster and could scale to far more data.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: What is the main 'catch' with attention?
choices: It only works in English
The cost grows with the square of the text length, so long inputs get expensive
It can't be sped up at all
It needs a human to label every word
why: Comparing every word to every other word means doubling the text roughly quadruples the work. That's why early models had short memories, and why making attention cheaper has been a major research focus ever since.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: In the study, who remembered more material a full week later?
choices: Students who re-read the passage
Students who tested themselves on it
Both groups remembered the same amount
Neither group remembered anything
why: After a week the self-testers held ~61% vs ~40% for re-readers — even though re-readers did slightly better on a test taken 5 minutes after studying.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: What is 'the testing effect'?
choices: Test anxiety lowering your score
Recalling information strengthens memory more than restudying it
Tests being unfair measures of knowledge
The boost you get from re-reading right before an exam
why: The testing effect is the finding that retrieving information from memory improves long-term retention more than passively reviewing it.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: Why do most people pick the less effective study method?
choices: It's faster
Re-reading feels fluent and confident while testing feels like struggling
Testing is more expensive
Teachers tell them to
why: Self-testers predicted they'd do worse, because retrieval feels effortful and exposes gaps — while re-reading feels smooth and reassuring. The comfortable method is the weaker one.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: What is the 'protein folding problem' AlphaFold tackled?
choices: Stopping proteins from breaking down
Predicting a protein's 3D shape from its amino-acid sequence
Counting how many proteins a cell makes
Translating DNA into amino acids
why: A protein's function comes from its folded 3D shape. Predicting that shape from just the order of amino acids — without measuring it in a lab — was a riddle biologists chased for about 50 years.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: How did AlphaFold use evolution as a clue to the shape?
choices: It evolved new proteins from scratch
It ignored other species entirely
Where two amino acids stayed in step across many related species, they're often touching in the folded shape
It measured how fast proteins mutate
why: AlphaFold gathers similar proteins from many species. Positions that changed together over evolution tend to be physically close in the 3D structure, which gives the model strong hints about how the chain folds.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: What is a real limitation of AlphaFold's predictions?
choices: It only works on human proteins
Its shapes are guesses that need checking, and it's weak on floppy proteins and protein motion
It takes years to run
It can only predict one protein ever
why: AlphaFold outputs a confident predicted resting shape even when it should be uncertain, so results need validation. It's strongest on single well-folded proteins and weaker on disordered ones, on how proteins assemble, and on the motion they use to do their jobs.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: What physically changes in the brain during sleep, according to this study?
choices: Brain cells multiply rapidly
The gaps between brain cells widen, letting cleaning fluid flow through more freely
The brain shrinks permanently
Blood stops flowing to the brain
why: In sleeping mice, the interstitial space — the gaps between brain cells — widened by about 60 percent. That opened the channels so cerebrospinal fluid could surge through and rinse out waste much faster than while awake.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: Why does the beta-amyloid result connect this study to Alzheimer's disease?
choices: Beta-amyloid is the protein that clumps into plaques in Alzheimer's, and sleep cleared it about twice as fast
Beta-amyloid only exists in people who sleep well
The study cured Alzheimer's in mice
Beta-amyloid makes you sleepy
why: Beta-amyloid is the sticky waste protein that accumulates as plaques in Alzheimer's. Since the brain cleared it roughly twice as fast during sleep, weak or missing sleep could let it build up — a plausible, still-unproven link to brain decline.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: What is the main limitation a careful reader should keep in mind?
choices: The study was never published
The findings come from mice and haven't been fully confirmed in living humans
It only looked at one mouse
It proved sleep loss causes Alzheimer's
why: The detailed measurements were made in mice, and confirming the same fluid dynamics in living people is hard. The work shows clearance speeds up during sleep, but it does not prove that poor sleep causes Alzheimer's — that remains a hypothesis under investigation.
